install.title: Install Blok
install.description: Add Blok to your project using your favorite package manager.

configure.title: Import and configure
configure.description: Import the editor and tools, then configure your block types.

save.title: Save content
save.description: Extract clean JSON data ready to save anywhere.

quickStart.title: Quick Start
quickStart.description: Get up and running with Blok in just a few simple steps.
quickStart.lead: This is the fastest path from an empty project to a working editor — install the package, mount it, and save your first block of content.
quickStart.checkpoint: If everything worked, you should now see an empty editor with one paragraph block, ready to type into.
quickStart.troubleshooting:
Nothing rendering, or a crash in the console? The most common cause is a missing container: `new Blok({ holder: 'editor' })` needs a `
` already on the page, or it throws `element with ID «editor» is missing`.

blokClass.title: Blok Class
blokClass.description: The main editor class that initializes and manages the Blok editor instance.

save.name: save()
save.description: Extracts the current editor content as structured JSON data. This is the primary method for persisting editor content.
save.note: Call after `await editor.isReady`. The returned JSON is your source of truth — store it and feed it back to `render()`.

render.name: render(data)
render.description: Renders editor content from previously saved JSON data.
render.note: Loads saved content and replaces the current document. To append instead of replace, use `blocks.insertMany()`.

focus.name: focus(atEnd?)
focus.description: Sets focus to the editor. Optionally positions cursor at the end of content.
focus.note: Pass `true` to place the caret at the very end. For a specific block or offset, use the `caret` API instead.

clear.name: clear()
clear.description: Removes all blocks from the editor.
clear.note: Wipes all blocks and leaves one empty paragraph. It's undoable — unlike `destroy()`, which tears the instance down.

destroy.name: destroy()
destroy.description: Destroys the editor instance and removes all DOM elements and event listeners.
destroy.note: Call from your framework's unmount hook to avoid leaked listeners. The instance is unusable afterwards — create a new `Blok` to start again.

isReady.name: isReady
isReady.description: Promise that resolves with the ready editor instance

configuration.title: Configuration
configuration.description: The configuration object passed to the Blok constructor.

placeholder.description: Placeholder text shown in the first block when the editor is empty; false disables it

minHeight.description: Height in px of the editor's bottom clickable zone

onChange.description: Change callback function; the event argument carries the mutation(s) that occurred (batched into an array when several fire at once)

onSave.description: Reactive save callback — fires automatically with the full serialized content on every debounced content change, so you don't have to call save() by hand.

onReady.description: Fires once when the editor becomes ready, receiving the fully-initialized Blok instance

autofocus.description: If true, sets the caret in the first block once the editor is ready

inlineToolbar.description: Default inline toolbar for all tools; an array restricts it to the listed inline tools, false disables it

theme.description: Color theme; 'auto' follows the OS preference via prefers-color-scheme

blocksApi.title: Blocks API
blocksApi.description: Manage blocks in the editor — create, delete, update, and reorder content.

clear.note: Same as the top-level `clear()`, exposed on the blocks module. Use when you already hold `editor.blocks`.

render.note: Replaces the whole document. To add blocks without clearing, use `insert()` or `insertMany()`.

renderFromHTML.note: Handy for importing legacy HTML, but the result is only as good as the tools' paste handlers — JSON via `render()` preserves more fidelity when you control the source.

delete.note: Without an index it deletes the focused block. Deleting the last remaining block automatically inserts a fresh empty paragraph, so the editor is never left without a caret target.

move.note: `toIndex` is in post-removal index space: for a forward move, subtract 1 or the block lands one slot too far.

getBlockByIndex.note: Returns `undefined` for out-of-range indexes — calling a method on an `undefined` result throws, so check the return value first.

getById.note: The stable way to reference a block across edits, since indexes shift. Returns `null` if the id no longer exists.

getCurrentBlockIndex.note: Reflects the block holding the caret. Returns `-1` when nothing is focused, so check before using it as an index.

getBlockIndex.note: Finds where a known block sits right now; pair with `getById()` when you have an id but need a position.

getBlockByElement.note: Bridges from a raw DOM node (e.g. a click target) back to its block — useful in custom event handlers.

getChildren.note: Returns the direct children of a container block (a column, toggle, or database). Empty array if it has none.

getBlocksCount.note: Counts every block including nested children (columns, toggles, table cells), not just top-level blocks. Use it for bounds before an index-based `insert()` or `move()`.

insert.note: Every parameter is optional — call `insert()` for an empty paragraph, or pass `type`/`data`/`index` for full control. Pass `replace: true` to overwrite the block at `index`.

data.description: Initial tool data for the new block.

config.description: Tool config for this block instance.

index.description: Position to insert the block at.

needToFocus.description: Whether to move focus to the inserted block.

replace.description: Replace the existing block at index instead of inserting around it.

id.description: Custom id for the new block.

tunes.description: Optional tune data applied at creation, keyed by tune name.

0.condition: No `type` is given and no `defaultBlock` is configured.
0.resolution: Pass an explicit `type`, or set `defaultBlock` in the editor config.

1.condition: The resolved tool name is not registered in the editor.
1.resolution: Register the tool in the editor's `tools` config before inserting a block of that type.

2.condition: `replace: true` is passed but no block exists at `index`.
2.resolution: Check `index` against `blocks.getBlocksCount()` before calling with `replace: true`.

insertMany.note: Bulk-insert in one operation (and one undo step) — far cheaper than looping `insert()` for large pastes or imports.

composeBlockData.note: Builds a tool's default data without inserting anything — useful when you need a valid empty payload for `insert()` or `update()`.

update.note: Patches data/tunes in place, keeping the block id and type. To change the type, use `convert()` instead.

id.description: Id of the block to update.

data.description: Partial data merged into the block's existing data.

tunes.description: Tune data merged into the block's existing tunes.

0.condition: No block exists with the given `id`.
0.resolution: Confirm the id with `blocks.getById()` before calling `update()`.

convert.note: Both the source and target tools must declare `conversionConfig`, otherwise the call throws. Preserves text where possible.

id.description: Id of the block to convert. Its tool must declare `conversionConfig.export`.

newType.description: Name of the registered tool to convert to. Its tool must declare `conversionConfig.import`.

dataOverrides.description: Data fields to overwrite on the resulting block after conversion.

0.condition: No block exists with the given `id`.
0.resolution: Confirm the id with `blocks.getById()` before calling `convert()`.

1.resolution: Register the target tool in the editor's `tools` config.

2.condition: The source tool has no `conversionConfig.export`, the target tool has no `conversionConfig.import`, or neither does.
2.resolution: Add a `conversionConfig` to whichever tool is missing one, or convert through an intermediate tool that supports both directions.

splitBlock.note: Use for caret-aware splits (e.g. Enter mid-text); the update + insert land as a single undo entry.

stopBlockMutationWatching.note: Niche: call before programmatically replacing a block's DOM to suppress spurious `block-changed` events; the next render re-arms it.

blockApi.title: BlockAPI
blockApi.description: Interface for working with individual blocks. Returned by blocks.getById(), blocks.getBlockByIndex(), and blocks.insert().

save.note: Returns just this block's data — handy for inspecting or persisting one block without `editor.save()`'s full walk.

validate.note: Runs the tool's `validate()` against given data; use it to reject empty or malformed blocks before saving.

call.note: Escape hatch to invoke a custom method your tool exposes — for behaviour outside the standard BlockTool API.

dispatchChange.note: Call when you mutate a block outside Blok's knowledge (e.g. an async update) so change events and CRDT sync fire.

getActiveToolboxEntry.note: Resolves which toolbox variant is active (e.g. Heading 1 vs 2) — useful for reflecting state in custom UI.

caretApi.title: Caret API
caretApi.description: Control cursor position and selection within the editor.

setToFirstBlock.note: `position` accepts `'start'`/`'end'`/`'default'`; returns `false` if there's no block to land on.

setToLastBlock.note: Common after appending content — drop the caret at the end of the new last block with `'end'`.

setToPreviousBlock.note: Moves relative to the focused block; returns `false` at the top edge, so use the return value to detect boundaries.

setToNextBlock.note: Mirror of `setToPreviousBlock()`; returns `false` at the bottom edge.

setToBlock.note: The most flexible setter — target by `BlockAPI`, id, or index. Prefer id/BlockAPI over index, which shifts as blocks move.

blockOrIdOrIndex.description: Target block, given as a `BlockAPI` instance, block id, or numeric index.

position.description: Where within the block to place the caret.

offset.description: Character offset from `position` within the target input.

0.condition: `blockOrIdOrIndex` does not resolve to an existing block (unknown id or out-of-range index).
0.resolution: Check the boolean return value; `setToBlock()` never throws, so a falsy result is the only signal the target wasn't found.

focus.note: Lightweight way to focus the editor without choosing a block; pass `true` for the end. Same as the top-level `focus()`.

updateLastCaretAfterPosition.note: Call after an async caret move so undo lands the caret where the user ended up, not where the op started.

eventsApi.title: Events API
eventsApi.description: Subscribe to and manage editor lifecycle events.

on.note: Subscribe to editor lifecycle/content events. Keep a reference to the same callback so you can `off()` it later.

off.note: Pass the exact same function reference you subscribed with — anonymous inline callbacks can't be removed.

emit.note: For tools/plugins to broadcast custom events on the editor bus; app code usually listens with `on()` rather than emits.

historyApi.title: History API
historyApi.description: Control undo/redo functionality for editor operations.

undo.note: Programmatic Ctrl/Cmd+Z. No-op when there's nothing to undo — gate custom buttons on `canUndo()`.

redo.note: Programmatic Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+Z. Gate custom UI on `canRedo()` so the button disables correctly.

canUndo.note: Use to enable/disable a custom undo button; pair with `undo()`.

canRedo.note: Use to enable/disable a custom redo button; pair with `redo()`.

clear.note: Drops the entire undo stack — call after loading fresh content so users can't undo back into the previous document.

save.note: Identical to `editor.save()`; provided for code that works through the `saver` module. Both await the full block tree.

selectionApi.title: Selection API
selectionApi.description: Work with text selection within the editor.

findParentTag.note: The standard way for inline tools to detect whether the cursor is already inside their tag (e.g. an `<a>` for a link).

expandToTag.note: Grow the selection to wrap a whole element — typically to select an existing mark before replacing or removing it.

setFakeBackground.note: Keep a visible 'selection' highlight while focus moves to your tool's input. Always pair with a later `removeFakeBackground()`.

removeFakeBackground.note: Tears down the fake highlight set by `setFakeBackground()`; call when your inline UI closes.

clearFakeBackground.note: Heavier reset than `removeFakeBackground()` — clears both DOM and internal flags if state got out of sync.

save.note: Stash the current range before focus leaves the document (e.g. opening a dialog), then bring it back with `restore()`.

restore.note: Re-applies the range saved by `save()` — the round-trip that lets inline tools survive a focus change.

stylesApi.title: Styles API
stylesApi.description: Access CSS class names for styling custom tools and UI elements.

toolbarApi.title: Toolbar API
toolbarApi.description: Control the block toolbar and its state.

close.note: Programmatically dismiss the block toolbar — e.g. after your custom action runs so the UI doesn't linger.

open.note: Force the toolbar open for the current block; it normally appears on hover/focus, so use sparingly.

toggleBlockSettings.note: Open/close the ☰ settings menu in code; pass an explicit boolean to set state rather than flip it.

toggleToolbox.note: Open/close the + insert (slash) menu programmatically; pass a boolean to force a state.

inlineToolbarApi.title: InlineToolbar API
inlineToolbarApi.description: Control the inline formatting toolbar (bold, italic, etc.).

close.note: Dismiss the formatting popover — useful after a custom inline tool applies its change.

open.note: Show the formatting popover over the current selection; a no-op without selected text.

show.note: Built-in toast for lightweight feedback. Set `type: 'confirm'` or `'prompt'` to collect a yes/no or text response.

type.description: Notification type. `confirm` and `prompt` add action buttons.

style.description: Built-in visual style for alert notifications.

time.description: Auto-dismiss delay in ms for every notification type (default 8000).

okText.description: Label for the confirm/submit button (`confirm`/`prompt` types only).

okHandler.description: Confirm/submit callback. Receives the click event for `confirm`, or the input value for `prompt`. Required for prompt notifications.

cancelText.description: Label for the cancel button (`confirm` type only).

inputType.description: HTML input type for the prompt's text field (`prompt` type only).

placeholder.description: Placeholder text for the prompt's input field (`prompt` type only).

default.description: Default value pre-filled in the prompt's input field (`prompt` type only).

0.condition: The notifier module fails to load (e.g. blocked by CSP, dynamic import failure).
0.resolution: `show()` never throws or rejects — check the browser console, since the failure is logged rather than propagated to your call site.

sanitizerApi.title: Sanitizer API
sanitizerApi.description: Clean and sanitize HTML content to prevent XSS attacks.

clean.note: Strip unwanted HTML against a rule set before inserting external content — your defence for paste and imported strings.

tooltipApi.title: Tooltip API
tooltipApi.description: Display tooltip hints on UI elements.

show.note: Imperatively show a tooltip for one-off cases. For hover behaviour prefer `onHover()`, which wires the listeners for you.

hide.note: Hide whatever tooltip is currently shown; safe to call even if none is visible.

onHover.note: The ergonomic choice — a tooltip that shows on hover and hides on leave without managing listeners yourself.

readOnlyApi.title: ReadOnly API
readOnlyApi.description: Control the read-only state of the editor.

set.note: The preferred way to enter/leave read-only mode. Returns a promise resolving to the new state once applied.

toggle.note: Kept for compatibility; without a parameter it flips the current state.

i18nApi.title: I18n API
i18nApi.description: Internationalization support for translating UI strings.

t.note: Look up a UI string from the active dictionary inside a tool, so your tool localises with the editor.

has.note: Check a key exists before translating, to fall back gracefully when a tool ships partial dictionaries.

getEnglishTranslation.note: Returns the English value regardless of locale — used to index content for multilingual search.

getLocale.note: Returns the active locale code (e.g. `'en'`) so a tool can branch on the editor's current language.

uiApi.title: UI API
uiApi.description: Access to Blok UI elements and state.

listenersApi.title: Listeners API
listenersApi.description: Manage custom DOM event listeners with automatic cleanup.

on.note: Managed `addEventListener` — Blok tracks the listener and removes it on `destroy()`, preventing leaks. Keep the returned id to remove it early.

off.note: Remove a managed listener by element + handler; or use `offById()` if you kept the id from `on()`.

offById.note: Remove a listener using the id `on()` returned — cleaner than matching element + handler again.

getBlockTools.note: Enumerate the registered block tools at runtime — handy for a custom block picker or debugging tool config.

outputData.title: OutputData
outputData.description: The data structure returned by the save() method.

blockData.title: BlockData
blockData.description: The structure of each block in the blocks array.

concepts.title: Everything is a block
concepts.description: Blok has one core idea. Understand it, and the rest of the API falls into place.
concepts.lead: In Blok, every piece of content is a block. There is no separate document model running alongside them — blocks are the universal primitive. A paragraph is a block. A heading is a block. A whole database, and every row inside it, is a block. Once this clicks, the API stops reading like a list of features and starts reading like one consistent tree.

shape.title: The shape of a block
shape.body: Every block — whatever its type — is the same small object. `type` names the tool that renders it, `data` holds that tool's content, and `tunes` holds optional block-level settings.

tree.title: Blocks form a tree
tree.body: Blocks aren't a flat list. Any block can contain other blocks through `contentIds`, and each child points back through `parentId`. A block that holds children is, in effect, a page — opening it means navigating into its children.

everywhere.title: The same idea, all the way up
everywhere.body: Features that look like separate systems are really just blocks arranged in the tree:

paragraph.why: Its `data` holds the text and inline formatting.

database.why: Its `data` holds the schema and view configs — not a side table.

row.why: It's a child of the database block; its values live in `data.properties`.

page.why: Any block with children. Opening it navigates into its `contentIds`.

notBlocks.title: What is not a block
notBlocks.body: Structured property values — a status, a priority, a due date on a row — are not blocks. They're metadata stored in the owning block's `data`. The rule of thumb: if it has an identity, stores content, and can be nested, it's a block; if it's a typed value that belongs to a block, it lives in that block's `data`.

why.title: Why this matters to you
why.body: Because everything is a block, you learn one set of moves and they work everywhere:

operations.label: One API for everything
operations.text: `insert`, `move`, and `delete` operate on any block — a paragraph, a row, or a whole database — with the same calls.

serialization.label: One save format
serialization.text: `editor.save()` walks the same tree for every kind of content, so persistence and rendering never need special cases.

nesting.label: One nesting model
nesting.text: `parentId` and `contentIds` describe containment everywhere, so a row inside a database and a paragraph inside a toggle behave the same way.

decision.title: Extending Blok? Ask one question
decision.body: Before designing a new feature, ask: “Is this a block?” If it represents content, data, or a container, the answer is almost always yes — build it as a block instead of inventing a parallel data model.

examples.calendar: A calendar view → a view config on a database block; the rows stay row blocks.
examples.toc: A table of contents → a block that reads its sibling blocks.

next.intro: Ready to work with the tree directly?
next.blocksSuffix: for inserting and moving blocks, and
next.blockDataSuffix: for the exact shape of a saved block.

tutorial.title: Build your first editor
tutorial.description: Mount Blok, capture some content, and save it as JSON you can store and load back — the full round-trip in five steps.
tutorial.lead: This is a hands-on walkthrough. By the end you'll have a working editor, you'll have saved its content to JSON, and you'll have loaded that JSON straight back in — the round-trip that everything else in Blok is built on.

mount.title: Mount the editor
mount.body: Point Blok at an element on your page and create an instance. Once `isReady` resolves you have a live editor with an empty paragraph waiting for the caret.

write.title: Write some content
write.body: Click in and type. Press Enter to start a new block, or press `/` to open the tool menu and choose a heading, list, or quote. Every line you create is a separate block.

save.title: Save it to JSON
save.body: Call `save()` to get your content as a plain object. There's no HTML to parse and no hidden state — just blocks you can store anywhere.
save.payoff: That object is the whole point. It's portable JSON you can drop into a database, a file, or an API — and it's exactly what you'll load back next.

render.title: Load it back
render.body: Hand the same object to `render()` and Blok rebuilds the content exactly. Save on unload, render on load — that's persistence, done.

tools.title: Add a tool
tools.body: Blok ships lean. Register the tools you want under the `tools` config and they appear in the `/` menu, ready to use.

checkpoint.title: Checkpoint: what success looks like
checkpoint.body: You should now have a live editor on the page, a `data` object in hand from `editor.save()` with your blocks inside it, and that same object loading cleanly through `editor.render()`. If all three are true, you've got the round-trip — everything else in Blok builds on it.

next.title: Where to go next
next.howTo: Build your own block from scratch — render, edit, and save like the built-ins.
next.concepts: Understand the one idea the whole API is built on.

howToCustomTool.title: Create a custom block tool
howToCustomTool.description: Build a block tool from scratch — a callout box that renders, edits, and saves like any built-in block.
howToCustomTool.lead: Every built-in block is just a tool that follows the `BlockTool` interface, and you can add your own. Here you'll build a Callout block: an editable box that saves its text and reloads it like any other block.

scaffold.title: Write the tool class
scaffold.body: A block tool needs three things: a static `toolbox` getter so it shows up in the `/` menu, a `render()` that returns the block's element, and a `save()` that returns the data to persist.

register.title: Register it in the editor
register.body: Pass your class under the `tools` config. The key you give it becomes the block's `type` in saved data.

use.title: Use it and save
use.body: Open the `/` menu, pick Callout, type into the box, and call `save()`. Your block appears in the output alongside the built-in ones.

further.title: Going further
further.body: Add a `validate()` method to drop empty blocks, a settings menu through `renderSettings()`, or paste handling with `onPaste`. The `BlockTool` interface covers the full lifecycle — `rendered()`, `updated()`, `moved()`, and `removed()`.
further.exampleNote: `validate()` runs before `save()` resolves, so an empty callout never makes it into the output. Tunes are opt-in per tool — register the tune class as a tool (a class with `static isTune = true`), then list its name in `tunes: [...]` and Blok adds it to the block's settings menu.

next.intro: For the complete tool contract, see the
next.suffix: for the exact shape your `save()` feeds into.

hero.title: Build beautiful
hero.description: A production-ready, extensible rich text editor that brings Notion-like block-based editing to your app. Customizable, themeable, and battle-tested.

features.description: Everything you need to ship a modern block editor: clean JSON, a full block library, and a deep, typed API.

cleanJson.title: Typed JSON, never HTML
cleanJson.description: Just typed JSON blocks, never an HTML blob.
cleanJson.learnMore: Learn more about Clean JSON output

details.summary: Blok is a headless editor built around a structured document model. Your app owns that data and decides how to store and display it.
details.benefit1: Typed JSON output, no HTML parsing
details.benefit2: Stable block IDs for diffing and version control
details.benefit3: Render on the server or the client
details.benefit4: Framework-agnostic core with full TypeScript types

blockLibrary.title: Every block, built in
blockLibrary.description: Notion-like blocks, ready in the box.
blockLibrary.learnMore: Learn more about the block library

details.summary: Blok includes the blocks most documents need, so you can put together rich content without building each one yourself.
details.benefit1: Tables with merged cells, resizing and cell colors
details.benefit4: Code, image, video, file and 100+ embeds

customBlocks.title: Bring your own blocks
customBlocks.description: Your own block tools, inline tools and block tunes.

details.summary: Built-in blocks cover most cases. When you need something specific, you can add your own without forking the editor.
details.benefit1: Custom block tools with a render/save API
details.benefit2: Custom inline tools and block tunes
details.benefit3: Lifecycle hooks: rendered, updated, moved, removed
details.benefit4: Public API for blocks, caret, history and selection

slashCommands.title: Type / to add anything
slashCommands.description: Press / or click + to open the toolbox, then search and insert any block — or use Markdown shortcuts like #, -, 1., [] and >.
slashCommands.learnMore: Learn more about the toolbox and Markdown

details.summary: Press / to open the toolbox — everything you can add: headings, lists, images, tables and more. Pick one without reaching for the mouse.
details.benefit1: Start typing to filter the toolbox to what you want
details.benefit2: Markdown shortcuts: # for a heading, - for a list, > for a quote
details.benefit3: Or click the + button on any empty line

tables.title: Spreadsheet-grade tables
tables.description: Merged cells, column resizing, cell colors, heading rows and full keyboard navigation.

details.summary: The table block stores its layout as data, so merged cells and column widths survive a save and reload.

embeds.title: Embed 100+ services
embeds.description: Paste a link to embed 100+ services like YouTube, Figma, Spotify and Gist, or get an OpenGraph bookmark card.

details.summary: Paste a link and Blok works out how best to display it.
details.benefit2: Video, audio, social, code and design sources
details.benefit3: OpenGraph bookmark cards as a fallback
details.benefit4: Notion-like paste menu: link, bookmark or embed

undoRedo.title: Conflict-free undo & redo
undoRedo.description: Yjs-backed history with ⌘Z / ⌘⇧Z. Conflict-free, grouped at word boundaries, and it restores your caret.
undoRedo.learnMore: Learn more about undo and redo

details.summary: History is built on a CRDT (Yjs), so undo and redo are conflict-free and behave predictably even across complex block operations.

languages.title: 68 languages, RTL-ready
languages.description: Lazy-loaded translations for 68 locales with right-to-left support. Only your language ships to the browser.

details.summary: The editor's own interface is translated, so your users see Blok in their language.
details.benefit3: Lazy-loaded for a minimal base bundle

media.title: Image, video & audio
media.description: Drop in images, a YouTube-grade video player and audio with live waveforms. Every one a first-class block.

details.summary: Each kind of media gets its own block, with controls suited to how people use it.
details.benefit2: Video player with scrubber, speed, PiP & theater
details.benefit3: Audio blocks with cached waveform peaks
details.benefit4: Paste or drag a file to upload

migrationCard.title: Migrate from EditorJS
migrationCard.description: Already using EditorJS? Our automated codemod handles most of the transition — just run it, review the changes, and ship.

docsCta.title: Explore the API reference
docsCta.description: Browse every module, method, and option — each with copy-paste examples and a guided sidebar.

frameworks.description: A framework-agnostic core with a fully typed API, first-class React, Vue, and Angular adapters, and a zero-build CDN script. Pick whichever fits your project.
frameworks.vanillaDescription: Point Blok at any DOM node and go. Fully typed, no framework needed.
frameworks.reactDescription: The useBlok hook owns the editor lifecycle; <BlokContent> mounts it. React 18 and 19.
frameworks.vueDescription: Same shape as React: useBlok drives the editor, <BlokContent> mounts it. Vue 3.
frameworks.angularDescription: Add <blok-editor> and bind your tools. Standalone component, Angular 20+.
frameworks.cdnDescription: Add one script tag and use the BlokEditor global. Good for prototypes and no-build pages.

whyBlok.description: Most editors hand you a bare core and a long plugin shopping list. Blok comes with the batteries included: typed JSON output, a full block library, and a deep API, all working out of the box.
whyBlok.disclaimer: Comparison reflects each library's out-of-the-box defaults; all are extensible via plugins.

trusted.title: Companies that trust us
trusted.summary: Blok powers the internal knowledge base behind one of the world's largest pizza chains. Thousands of authors, one block model.
trusted.contactLead: We'll help you set it up and adapt it to exactly what you need.

footer.tagline: A friendly block-based rich text editor for modern applications.
footer.author: JackUait

search.noResultsDescription: Try different keywords or browse the documentation sections
search.emptyDescription: Find API methods, configuration options, tools, and more

editorAccess.heading: Reaching the editor instance
editorAccess.vanilla: The methods below run on the editor you created with new Blok(). They are available once editor.isReady resolves.
editorAccess.react: The methods below run on the editor returned by useBlok(). It is null until the editor mounts, so guard the value with optional chaining before calling in.
editorAccess.vue: The methods below run on the editor returned by useBlok(). It is a ref that stays null until the editor mounts, so unwrap it with .value before calling in.
editorAccess.angular: The methods below run on the live editor. Grab it from the component's (ready) output, then call any method shown below.

demo.title: Try the
demo.subtitle: A fully interactive editor running right here in your browser. Type
demo.subtitleRest: for commands, drag blocks to reorder, or select text to format.
demo.hintInstantFeedbackDesc: Everything happens locally in your browser. No server roundtrips, just pure performance.
demo.hintCleanJsonDesc: Content is stored as structured JSON blocks. Parse, validate, and store anywhere.
demo.hintBlockArchDesc: Create custom block types with simple JavaScript classes. Fully extensible.
demo.outputInitialMessage: Click "Get JSON" to see the output

editorWrapper.buildHint: Make sure the Blok editor is built with

demo.welcomeParagraph: This is a live demo of the Blok editor. Try typing / to see available commands, or select text to format it.
demo.welcomeListItem3: Drag and drop to reorder blocks

migration.heroDescription: One codemod rewrites imports, types, selectors and configuration. Saved content loads as-is — anything without a Blok equivalent is logged, never silently lost.
migration.aliasNoteDescription: Blok is exported as the default export and under an EditorJS alias, so you can keep your existing variable name and only change the import path. import Blok, import { Blok } and import { EditorJS } all resolve to the same class.
migration.aliasNoteCode: import { EditorJS } from '@jackuait/blok'; // new EditorJS({ ... }) stays unchanged
migration.customInlineToolAfter: render() { return { icon, name, onActivate, isActive }; }
migration.customInlineToolFastPathNote: Keep an existing EditorJS inline tool without a rewrite by wrapping its class. The shim routes onActivate → surround, isActive → checkState, forwards the static title/shortcut/sanitize and pulls the icon from the legacy render(). Without it, Blok silently skips a legacy HTMLElement render() and the tool vanishes. renderActions() is surfaced as a popover panel and clear() runs when the popover closes; the native MenuConfig rewrite is still recommended for full control.
migration.customBlockToolNote: render, save, validate, renderSettings, merge, onPaste and the toolbox/conversionConfig statics are read by the same keys. Optionally add setReadOnly(state) for cheaper read-only toggling.
migration.droppedFieldsWarning: When auto-migration drops a field with no Blok equivalent, Blok emits a console.warn prefixed [Blok migration] naming the block and field, so the loss is visible rather than silent. Capture any of these before migrating if you depend on them.
migration.droppedFieldsQuoteFields: alignment (caption is auto-migrated into a following paragraph)
migration.codemodApplyLabel: Apply the changes to your files
migration.codemodVerboseDescription: Show detailed output for each file processed
migration.supportedVersionsTarget: Blok targets the Editor.js 2.x line. The codemod is built against typical 2.x package versions (editorjs ^2.28, header ^2.8, paragraph ^2.11, list ^1.9) and the runtime reads the saved-data shapes those versions produce.
migration.supportedVersionsStubNote: Any block type Blok doesn't recognize and has no registered tool for renders as a stub and logs a "Tool «name» is not found" warning. Its data is preserved, so registering the tool later restores the block.
migration.sectionCodemodDescription: One command rewrites imports, types, selectors and configuration. Preview first, then apply.
migration.sectionChangesDescription: Every rename the codemod performs, before and after.
migration.sectionCssDescription: Blok replaces Editor.js class names with data attributes. Map any custom styles with this reference.
migration.sectionToolsDescription: Block tools port unchanged. Inline tools return a MenuConfig — or wrap the old class and skip the rewrite.
migration.sectionVerifyDescription: Coverage is per block/tool, not per Editor.js point release — what matters is which tools your content uses.
migration.compatGroupDropInHint: These tools load your saved data as-is.
migration.compatGroupAutoHint: Converted at runtime, by the codemod, or both.
migration.compatGroupNotBundledHint: Blok ships no equivalent — register your own tool or keep the Editor.js one.
